title: Surówka z kapusty
subtitle: Polish white cabbage salad with caraway and vinegar
excerpt: Raw white cabbage shredded finely, then dressed while salted and slightly softened with caraway seeds, vinegar, and a small amount of sugar. The standard cold side dish of the Polish table — sharp, crunchy, and made in a few minutes.
sourceNotes: Surówka z kapusty is the everyday raw cabbage salad of Polish home cooking, appearing in household cookery books throughout the twentieth century. Caraway seeds and cider vinegar are the typical seasonings; some cooks add a grated carrot for colour. It should not be confused with kapusta kiszona (sauerkraut), which is fermented.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps refrigerated for up to 2 days once dressed. The cabbage softens further over time; dress a day ahead if you prefer a less crunchy result.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Salt the cabbage up to an hour ahead. Dress up to 2 days ahead for a softer result.

content.text: White cabbage surówka is not a salad in the dressed-leaves sense. It is salted first, which draws moisture from the cabbage and changes its texture from raw-crunchy to something closer to the lightly softened crunch of coleslaw — without the heaviness of mayonnaise. The result is sharp, slightly sweet, slightly sour, and goes with almost anything. It is present on the Polish dinner table as automatically as bread.

Shred the cabbage as thinly as possible. A sharp knife works, but a mandoline or the slicing disc of a food processor makes the job faster and more even. Thicker shreds are tougher and take longer to soften under the salt.

items.prepNote: about 400 g, core removed and finely shredded

content.text: Toss the shredded cabbage with {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Squeeze firmly with your hands for a minute, then leave for 30 minutes. The cabbage will release liquid and soften noticeably. Drain off the liquid and squeeze out as much remaining moisture as you can.

Stir in the grated carrot if using. Whisk together the {{caraway-seeds}}, {{cider-vinegar}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, and {{sunflower-oil}}. Pour over the cabbage and toss well. Taste and adjust with a little more vinegar or a pinch of salt. Serve immediately for maximum crunch, or refrigerate for 30 minutes for a softer texture.

items.cause: Shredded too thickly or not salted long enough
items.fix: Shred as thinly as possible and extend the salting time to an hour, squeezing again halfway through

items.symptom: Watery dressing at the bottom of the bowl
items.cause: Cabbage not fully squeezed after salting
items.fix: Squeeze the cabbage firmly in small handfuls before adding the dressing. Excess moisture is the main cause of a diluted surówka

items.cause: Too many seeds for the amount of cabbage
items.fix: Reduce to a quarter teaspoon, or omit caraway and season with a small amount of celery salt instead

content.text: Surówka is the Polish category name for all raw, dressed vegetable salads. Kapusta surówka is made year-round because white cabbage keeps well through the autumn and winter. It sits alongside almost every main course in the Polish home kitchen: kotlety schabowe, pierogi, bigos, and roast pork all appear with it. Caraway seeds appear in Polish and Czech cooking as a standard seasoning for cabbage, a pairing that goes back centuries in Central European cookery.
